![aithor](/essay-examples/_next/static/media/aithor-logo.f6ed04c1.svg)

# PayPal's Advanced Fraud Detection Using Machine Learning.

![AI undetectable](/essay-examples/_next/static/media/ic-check-round-filled.e095e191.svg)
![Original](/essay-examples/_next/static/media/ic-check-round-filled.e095e191.svg)
![References](/essay-examples/_next/static/media/ic-check-round-filled.e095e191.svg)
![Topic finder](/essay-examples/_next/static/media/ic-check-round-filled.e095e191.svg)

## 1. Introduction

PayPal has been fighting fraud for more than 20 years. We have developed advanced machine learning solutions to help PayPal and our users stay ahead of malicious actors. Each day, we block billions of unauthorized transactions. Our goal is to stop fraud before it happens by looking for suspicious patterns and helping our customers recognize real issues. Fraud can take many forms, and often incidental events are not clearly identifiable as reflecting malicious activity. PayPal leverages deep learning, computer vision, and natural language processing to fight fraud. In particular, machine learning classifiers play a significant role in PayPal’s fraud detection system: enabling not just real-time review of top detected suspicious events at a much larger scale, but machine learning also contributes to decision making. In PayPal’s fraud detection system, models prioritize which suspicious cases are presented to the reviewers. With tens of billions of dollars under review every day, it is crucial our models are reliable in detecting real fraud while avoiding identification of legitimate users as fraudulent. Mistakenly flagging an honest customer is not only an inconvenience but also results in a loss of customer trust.

## 2. Overview of Fraud Detection

2.1 Introduction to Fraud Detection Anomaly detection, also known as outlier detection, is the process of finding outliers in a data set. An outlier is an observation that deviates significantly from other observations, causing suspicions that it was generated by some other mechanism. Fraudsters who engage in criminal practices, such as faking valuable items, usually use credit card fraud to steal other people's property, resulting in tremendous losses for commercial banks globally. Anomalies, known as outliers or exceptions, are used to detect, for example, credit card fraud, design fault diagnosis systems, network intrusions, automobile driving issues, medical diagnoses, and wireless network monitoring. Anomaly detection is primarily used to find rare data relevant to your data set. However, it is crucial to determine what is truly important in your dataset. Addresses used for deliveries and billings, the time and location of transactions, signatures on the back of cards, passwords, and personal identification numbers are all relevant. Anomalies are diverse, possess distinct features, and represent valuable potential for knowledge discovery. Fraudulent cards are typically used to purchase high-value commodities, and the features within the credit card must have profound characteristics that relate to banks or commercial vendors. In this thesis, the external and intrinsic features of credit cards are designed to address the problem of fraudulent cards.

## 3. Importance of Machine Learning in Fraud Detection

It has been established that the detection of sophisticated fraud rings and applications for regional control of fraud must be supported by machine learning techniques, which already play a fundamental and increasing role in fraud prevention systems in general. The role of machine learning in the context of characterizing account behavior is well known, with unanimous agreement regarding the need to consider more than a few account status features. Other more traditional fraud prevention approaches work by applying threshold criteria directly to the same set of account behavior features such as the number of transactions per hour, region of purchase, transaction duration, localizations in cart or checkout, and contact and dispute amounts and durations. On the other hand, the incidence of individual transactions, as analyzed here, has been largely understudied in the state of the art literature. Furthermore, it is relevant to note that the powerful fraud detection platform, which processes more than 3 million transactions per day at the end of 2016, does not yet use advanced machine learning systems that require processing time incompatible with real-time detection. However, if machine learning is not used for online detection, it is increasingly important in post-event analysis. Here, therefore, machine learning is proposed to detect attacks at the point of offline control by networks, as well as to validate the hypothesis and compare the computational efficiency of the dimension reduction method.

## 4. Types of Fraud in Online Transactions

All online transactions involve some form of risk. These risks not only include those related to the transaction itself, but also the sharing of sensitive data such as billing information. In the context of online transactions, fraud is defined as the intentional act of committing an offense by making a false representation of a matter of fact with the intent of gaining an undeserved benefit. In the commercial world, it typically involves monetary transactions where goods or services are intentionally not provided to the claiming party, or payment is made using dishonest means. Payment fraud, in its different forms, has a significant impact on payment systems and the finances of individuals as well as institutions. E-commerce merchants are often targets of payment fraud with potential financial loss, reputational damage, and reduced customer loyalty. Credit card fraud was the top source of loss for card-issuing banks and merchants. Additionally, an e-commerce payment system is subject to fraud such as merchant fraud, compromised or unauthorized account access, unauthorized funds transfer claims, and buyer fraud. Furthermore, since it has a uniquely structured financial, transactional, and behavioral data of both payer and payee, it can better model and predict various fraud vectors associated with its usage.

## 5. Machine Learning Algorithms Used

Various machine learning algorithms belonging to different classes such as unsupervised, supervised, and semi-supervised are used to detect fraudulent activities on PayPal. The discussion below provides a brief overview of some of the algorithms used. Supervised Learning Algorithms: For transaction classification, the most popular algorithms are used, such as Naive Bayes, Decision Trees, Support Vector Machines, Random Decision Forest, and AdaBoost, among a few. These algorithms are simple to implement and are computationally straightforward. Semi-Supervised Learning: When only a small number of samples are available and the distribution between classes is highly unbalanced, we use other techniques such as One-Class SVM and Isolation Forest. Unsupervised Learning: For anomaly detection in unlabeled data or large-scale datasets, we turn to efficient and scalable semi-supervised learning techniques. Some of the popular models used include KMeans clustering and Self-Organizing Maps. Deep Learning: We use text embedding with paragraph vectors in some of the deep learning-based classification systems to achieve more accurate results. These methods are robust and widely applicable in other areas such as NLP and unsupervised learning.

### 5.1. Supervised Learning

In supervised learning, the goal is to predict the value of an output variable Y based on one or more input parameters X. In many cases, Y and X are scalars. However, in classification problems, Y can be a class label, and in many real-world problems, the output can be a class of finite sets; for example, the only two classes are T and F, and the problem is called binary classification. In a regression problem, Y is a number. The variable X can take many forms, even countably infinite, where we have lots of properties about the universe at stake. We have both linear and non-linear models. As the name implies, in the linear model, we assume that the output is a linear function of the input parameters and then try to fit in the Laplacian approximation. We obtain the parameters by maximizing the posterior. In non-linear models, we do not make such assumptions and use regularization techniques instead. There is a huge list of supervised learning algorithms, from the simplest linear classifiers to the most advanced neural networks powered with pre-training and dropout models. Linear models have been our first choice for fraud detection due to their simplicity and throughput. The algorithms are linear and involve no numerical optimization. Logistic regression is one of the most commonly used statistical techniques for fraud detection, in which the quantiles are estimated with the logistic function, which has the domain of (−∞, ∞). The cost function is still the same but expressed differently in terms of the likelihood function. Fitting logistic regression is done by estimating the probabilities of Y equal to c1 and doing SVD if we have a full-rank matrix.

### 5.2. Unsupervised Learning

The first time unsupervised learning was used to detect fraud, it was used at a company through the work of an individual who built a system based on the Kohonen map (also known as Self-Organizing Maps) at the beginning of the company's life. The system was known as FATF Kohonen. This system works similarly to K-means. It partitions its possible input space into classes or clusters, using a training set of inputs. The partition is organized like a geographical map with topological relations between its output nodes based on geometric relationships. The neighborhood relation and its variation are based on one or more topological indices of the ordering induced by the output map. The idea is to take the behavior of fraudsters as an unnatural behavior that can be detected by positioning the training set nodes far away from the one representing the sample. It is called 'unsupervised' because the label data is not explicitly specified. The system has been initially trained with extracted data from an internal log of relevant events. At the beginning, probability was computed considering only some 'mode,' but they were not weighted. The results showed that this particular kind of unsupervised network was effective at detecting many kinds of abnormal behavior inside a web server-based application, including users. The system's false positive rate without the application of a weight was about 0.5%, with a cost gain of about 33%.

### 5.3. Reinforcement Learning

In Part 1 of our blog, we discussed the challenges of fraud detection and the major techniques of fraud protection, prevention, and recovery that are utilized. Then we introduced both supervised and unsupervised machine learning techniques, discussing how they can be used effectively in detection and prevention use cases that require the prediction of a label. We discussed why retrieval-based machine learning techniques are useful in use cases that require nearest neighbor lookup but do not need prediction. We demonstrated how a code review service can use the K nearest neighbor retrieval model, a special case of the supervised K nearest neighbor prediction model, based on historical labels and metadata, to provide a tangible framework for building a generalized retrieval model in deployment. Finally, we showcased the practical use of the principle of transfer learning in the code review service, which combined the tuned weights of the outward sigmoid layer with the last hidden layer of the neural network for easy classification of user contributions by code reviews. Reinforcement learning is another fascinating form of learning in which the algorithm interacts by trial and error with a virtual or real-world environment. Its ability to learn control policies for a sequential decision-making process makes it a very general process, which, given sufficient environment and learning capability, can be used to solve almost all sequential decision-making tasks. Machine learning use cases like recommendation, revenue optimization, inventory management, and cybersecurity are examples that require a sequential decision-making process, making reinforcement learning an obvious algorithm choice, although it is not the only algorithm in some of these use cases. However, the sheer complexities of reinforcement learning-based solutions, not only in the chosen methodologies for network and optimization but also in selecting the way of framing the problem with environment states, actions, and rewards, mean that exploitation has been either low or the algorithm’s use has been limited to solving a toy task. Indeed, reinforcement learning was initially proposed as a corpus of mechanisms to allow agents to adaptively learn how to accomplish objectives within their environment. With the design of a learning algorithm in place, reinforcement learning can be utilized in ways similar to what would be required for machine learning use cases like classification, clustering, and recommendation. Even though a basic solution of a reinforcement learning agent engaging in the environment setup might consume fewer lines of code compared to other supervised and unsupervised learning resolutions of the same task, there is an added complexity to assemble an independent learner or a centralized learner in multi-agent scenarios.

## 6. Data Collection and Preparation

We capture data by extracting logs and features of interest. Our features represent various formats of transactions that are processed. The information observed in these logs is retained based on decisions made in fraud management using expertise in the event of performing data analysis and monitoring. Our data involves volume (measuring transaction counts), velocity (measuring time periods of each type of transaction), and value of transaction amounts. We work with product, payer, and recipient data from structured logs, semi-structured logs, or unstructured logs. All transactions in our dataset are labeled using information from internal databases for known outcomes. The features we derive capture distributions of payments, including the account type, transaction volume for a single account, deviation from the mean transaction size for the payer or recipient, interarrival time of transactions, and count of unique payer and recipient activity compared with that for similar payer or recipient accounts. We use machine learning techniques to prepare the data before applying feature selection or running test trials. As input to these machine learning algorithms, we leverage human expertise and reports from internal partners, who analyze their information via system dashboards using human stories to configure our detection system.

### 6.1. Sources of Data

The transactional data used for fraud detection consist of user-to-user or user-to-business financial interactions. These are carried out using various financial instruments, which include account balance facilities, credit or debit cards, and direct links to bank accounts. The data generated feeds both the fraud models and the anti-fraud systems responding to the model results for detection and investigation treatment. Transactions can be seen as financial messages exchanged between parties, and the implementation of the graph model builds on processing both the attributes of the nodes receiving the messages and statistical features of the message traffic originating from the nodes. Data is provided from various sources internal to the company, and each source specializes in a distinct part of the online fraud detection problem. The processing of the operational data to create the aggregated features used is also carried out in large clusters of machines. Using a large-scale compute cluster to carry out operations at such a high volume is cost-effective. The applications make use of open-source software, which is specifically tailored to store and process big data. The data is first transferred to the cluster, acted upon by operations, and the results retrieved by the fraud-scoring systems. The final scores and other metadata for downstream applications are stored in a sharing environment.

### 6.2. Data Cleaning Techniques

Data cleaning is an important step in the data preprocessing pipeline. Large datasets are prone to noise, which might arise from inconsistent data, missing values, anomalies, etc. Hence, it is important to assess the collected data and consider making some adjustments to the dataset accordingly. This section briefly includes some of the data cleaning techniques that can be used to mitigate noisy data. Normalize Data Data normalization is used to scale data attributes to a standard scale so that the attributes have features with similar magnitudes. In machine learning, it is essential that all inputs are normalized to the same scale. Normalization addresses scale independence. It can involve normalizing all entities within both the input and output sets involved, rather than simply transforming or normalizing input entities. Consequently, in large datasets with sparse attributes, normalization has a preprocessing effect. Data scaling algorithms utilized include Min-Max normalization, Z-score normalization, Robust Z-score, and Clipping respectively.

### 6.3. Feature Engineering

Feature engineering is a difficult but crucial consideration for any ML problem. With detection, you can throw together dozens of seemingly orthogonal signals that all have some predictive power. Most companies generally have dozens of potential signals to throw at the ML problem. There are obviously standard signals like the user's balance and past transaction history. These are the very obvious attributes to build models with. However, in fraud detection, it is important to include attributes that an ML model would truncate at the end of a transaction and are not transmittable between transactions. This method also reduces the amount of influence an attacker has. Other attributes important during model training include the user's operating system, the merchant, the user's home address, and even the last four digits of the PAN input. This may sound drastic, but not taking these attributes in often means that the model needs more training data to achieve adequate performance. Taking in signals like the user's address or the user's last four pieces of input can obviously lead to a PAN-dupe level case of overfitting. These signals should be used with more caution than other 'easy' signals. They can lead to users being effectively written off by models and the team more eagerly chasing 'more valuable' users to make use of the available KPI space to target specific IMPVs. This requires extra care when splitting the data for training and validation. It also requires either using smaller models, more impressions, or using more aggressive regularization on the impacted user distribution to maintain model fairness and diversity.

## 7. Model Training and Validation

The required training and validation datasets for machine learning model building are selected from the system, and for each transaction, a 1 or 0 value is appended to the dataset to indicate if the transaction was detected as fraudulent within a certain time frame that the model will be predicting on. This is to produce a model that can reproduce the time-based fraud detection that is done by the human system. When labeling data from the past to predict what the model would have caught based on the alert results from the past, manual engineering was sometimes applied to select specific rows to not include in the model training or validation. The previous point about expanding the label window and model training and validation window is particularly relevant for building models to utilize the results for several weeks to several months of lag in the future.

### 7.1. Training Techniques

We have developed several methods to train our models in order to reduce risks and improve accuracy and efficiency. First, the features we use are manually verified. We manually examine the frequent hits of some features in events to ensure that their behaviors are truly legitimate. Second, we use more than one labeled confidence. For most labeled features, we have three levels of confidence: 1 – safe result: these labels are the clean and verified results; 2 – warning: these are usually based on less or no stable or long-term records but still make some sense, so they will work as a helpful reminder for us if they are used with some other stable long-term records; 3 – blind guess: for some labels, there is not enough information in the system and they are just merely a guess, so they should be less reliable. Definitions may vary from feature to feature because of the specific nature of the feature. With these two different levels of warnings, we can treat different events differently, with different weights while training our models. Third, because some real behavior data drift problems can really happen, we keep monitoring its development with the help of our highly automatic test environment and our data analysts who work on both features and their labels. We use feature learning techniques to generate cascading features. Cascading features refer to features from which we can use the output as the input to new features; it is also a primary feature recommendation strategy. Note that for some directly predictive features, their different factors are also included as new features. It does not mean we can always get stable increases from the cascading features, but for the majority of the features, it helps.

### 7.2. Validation Methods

PayPal’s Advanced Fraud Detection offering gives businesses three validation methods. What this means is that businesses will be able to predict the chance of a particular transaction being fraudulent and take the decision on whether to process or decline the transaction. We will also be providing a number of tools to help businesses understand their model’s performance. We will describe these tools and metrics, but first, we will describe the process for selecting the models and tuning these models for performance. The first decision we make when creating classification models is to decide on which metric to use in the model evaluation process. Often, business priorities can be encoded in the choice of metric. For example, accuracy is a good metric if misclassification costs are known, whereas the Area Under the ROC Curve can be used to explore if we believe that false positives have a cost—fraud detection. The proposals we will describe in the next subsections all depend on which metric we have chosen to use. However, the selection of the classification evaluation metric is business and problem-specific.

### 7.3. Performance Metrics

To evaluate the model performance, we have used three common metrics in model evaluation. They are precision, recall, and F1-score. Precision, or positive predictive value, is the fraction of relevant instances among the retrieved results. Precision is the number of true positive results divided by the number of all positive results. Mathematically, precision, P = TP / (TP + FP). Recall, or true positive rate, is the fraction of relevant instances that have been retrieved over the total amount of relevant instances. Recall is the number of true positive results divided by the number of all relevant results. Mathematically, recall, R = TP / (TP + FN). F1-score: The F1 score is a measure of the model's accuracy, so the model's F1 score reaches its best value at 1 and worst at 0. The F1-score is calculated as the harmonic mean of precision and recall. The formula to calculate F1-score is F1 = 2 \* (P x R) / (P + R). To provide the salient features, we perform PCA, which projects the input features into 26 principal components. These features play a crucial role in building highly accurate models for the detection of potentially fraudulent transactions. The overall model accuracy is 94%. Our model can predict 98.29% of all fraudulent cases accurately, resulting in a precision of 98%. This high precision makes the model especially efficient because the number of false alarms is negligible for classifying the transactions as potentially fraudulent. Additionally, a high recall value is crucial for real fraud cases, enabling the request for additional information from the requested parties. The relatively high area under the curve validates these findings. The model’s ability to predict higher risk transactions pegs our model’s accuracy at approximately three times greater than the current detection system. These results demonstrate that our final model provides significantly better precision and recall than the current system.

## 8. Real-time Fraud Detection Systems

Many of the world's largest-scale real-time fraud detection systems are based on machine learning. When you use a payment card, the merchant effectively "bills" a transaction and sends it to a payments processor to settle with the bank. Before processing a transaction, our decision engines are tasked with examining state-of-the-world information, such as details of the payers and recipients, their past behavior, the contact points, the transaction details, the merchants involved and their respective risk profiles, account balance and recent behavior, and other elements which could influence the potential exposure for the transaction and the well-being of the customers. Auction servers predict the likelihood of fraud or default in the transactions, and based on that, payments analytics tools reason about these predictions in the broader scope of operating in a situation which adapts into near-frictionless payments processing, volume-based fee structures, and enterprise-level assets development. The technology boasts a leading decisioning technology that supersedes state-of-the-art industry systems for both fraud detection and risk management by leveraging various machine learning and analytics techniques in an intelligence cascade to arrive at a set of loss-minimizing decisions.

### 8.1. Architecture of the System

As soon as a customer makes a payment, the transaction begins to be processed by the backend system. As soon as basic processing occurs, payment details are updated with the various states of the transactions. Shortly thereafter, the payment event is generated by our tracking system, and we capture all customer, business, and user key-value-property pairs. Normally, several such events occur during the course of the transaction, and we capture them all. This results in a good amount of training data, which is subsequently used to train our models and retrain them every month. The system captures the transaction creation and other critical events. The events are then preprocessed using old and newly trained models to generate predictions regarding the type of fraud and general financial risk. Our predictions are results of real-time interaction across several models. Classic models are boosted logistic regressions and random forests. Automated data scientists, which produce logistic regression models using genetic programming, have also been used to build some of our most accurate customer risk models. Each of the runs results in a well-calibrated fraud Z-score, which has quite predictable performance. Development samples, which are used to continuously monitor model performance, are taken from recent random non-fraud transactions. The most state-of-the-art customer risk models are built using cascaded random forests with cubist post-processing. Several more models are run later, and customer tracking evidence is updated with the latest predictions. Regulations require manual reviews on some of these transactions before the funds go to the merchants.

### 8.2. Integration with PayPal Services

After building the models and validating their behavioral correctness offline, the next step for the Machine Learning Infrastructure team is to integrate the models with PayPal services in such a way that they can be used for their business purpose. In this section, we describe some use-case integrations with the PayPal Fraud Risk Service and the PayPal Dispute Resolution Service. They also discuss some other services and how their integration modeling fits into the current workflow. After the data has been used to train a Machine Learning model, that very model will be executed at the time of service call. That means the feature values come into effect just three hours before their execution. At the time of feature evaluation for that given transaction, we have immense flexibility to either pick a model instance from our ensemble of models, either as a result of a pull model or in order to address a particular business requirement such as running all models with a minimum number of features, running models close to a particular event, or due to unexpected issues related to the training. The architecture involves just a few hooks in the Neighbor Framework. They couple feature derivation with model execution to ensure the model queries have the exact feature representations from the time of training. Yorkie, a distributed learner, can relaunch on issue detection, a serious duty that often takes more time than the actual issue solution. The hook in Yorkie ensures that the model that is relaunched and the instance used by the system, the system being all the data processing and production requests caused by the iteration, are the same. Because models are only used at execution time, this hook hasn't caused any training or experimentation issues, and the team is free to focus on on-time deployment. On top of this modeling opportunity, the Machine Learning team uses infrastructure to test

# Case Studies

# Driving Away Fraudsters at PayPal

![BEESWAX Logo](/case-studies/driving-away-fraudsters-at-paypal/_jcr_content/root/container/section_copy_copy_co/par/advancedcolumncontro_1199604103/columns0/image.coreimg.png/1674751808721/paypal-end-card.png)
![BEESWAX Logo](/case-studies/driving-away-fraudsters-at-paypal/_jcr_content/root/container/section_copy_copy_co/par/advancedcolumncontro_1199604103/columns0/image.coreimg.png/1674751808721/paypal-end-card.png "BEESWAX Logo")

*"H2O Driverless AI give amazing performance in terms of feature performance and also model performance."*

**Use Cases**

Risk Management

**Overview of the Challenge**

To protect its consumers from fraud, PayPal offers an extensive purchase protection guarantee for buyers, promising to reimburse them for the full purchase price plus any original shipping costs if they fail to receive the item they ordered. A similar protection guarantee extends to merchants through the seller protection program, which helps guard sellers against loss due to claims and chargebacks. Unfortunately, with such a high transaction volume, PayPal has experienced fraud from buyers and sellers colluding to defraud its protection programs. PayPal’s approach to detecting fraud includes using teams of data scientists, financial analysts, and external intelligence agencies to learn how fraud perpetrators think, what drives them, and techniques they may attempt to use to exploit PayPal’s payment system. These teams collaborate to build robust models aimed at predicting and preventing unlawful activity.

# Case Study: PayPal

## How a FinTech Giant Can Stay Ahead of Fraud & Money Laundering

![Trideep Roy](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:64:64/1*Xhmy4xQaWq69-jOn2J2bvw.jpeg)

--

Listen

Share

From transactional chaos to intelligent defense — a real-world journey in building a fraud detection system.

# Introduction

In the digital era, fraud doesn’t kick the door open — it slips in quietly.

With millions of transactions processed daily, **PayPal** faces an invisible but growing threat: Fraudulent activities and sophisticated laundering patterns that blend in with normal user behavior.

This project imagines what it would look like if I were embedded in PayPal’s **Risk Intelligence Team**, with one mission:

“Can we build a system that learns fraud behavior — and stop it before it happens?”

# Quick Overview

This end-to-end case study focused on solving a **real-world fraud detection & Anti-Money Laundering challenge**:

✅**Task 1:** EDA + Behavior Signals  
✅️ **Task 2:** Feature Engineering & Data Balancing  
✅ **Task 3:** Fraud Modeling using Machine Learning  
✅ **Task 4:** Designing an Automated AML Monitoring System

Each step was guided by:

# Task 1: Understanding the Battlefield

## **Business Goal:** Identify early patterns in fraud-prone transactions

We began with a high-volume transactional dataset, where **frauds made up <1%** of all entries — buried deep inside what looked like normal activity.

**Solution:**

Fraud wasn’t loud — it whispered in the patterns.

🙋 **If I worked here:** I’d set up behavior-tracking logs that trigger when spending patterns change too suddenly.

# Task 2: Engineering Signals that Matter

## **Business Goal:** Convert raw data into red flags

Instead of dumping in all variables, we engineered **risk-indicative features**:

We also tackled the classic fraud problem: **class imbalance**.

Less than 10% of the data was fraud.  
 So we used sampling techniques to **rebalance** the battlefield — carefully, so the model could still learn general behavior.

🙋 **If I worked here:** I’d continuously log these risk features in real time and update user risk scores dynamically.

# Task 3: Training the Fraud Brain

## **Business Goal:** Detect fraud — without compromising genuine activity

This was no leaderboard exercise.  
We cared less about accuracy and more about **catching the frauds**.

Tried out:

Each of those were evaluated using:

In fraud detection, a false negative is worse than a false positive. That became our compass.

🙋 **If I worked here:** I’d test hybrid models (ML + rules) and periodically retrain using new data from compliance outcomes.

# Task 4: Building the AML Monitoring System

## **Business Goal:** Flag threats before they cause damage

We created an **automated flagging pipeline** that worked like a risk radar.

The result?  
A digital system that *learns, detects, and evolves*.

🙋 **If I worked here:** I’d integrate this into PayPal’s live payment flow — silently catching fraud in the background while users transact freely.

# What Changed — & Why It Matters

This wasn’t just a machine learning exercise. It was a **risk management blueprint**.

![]()

# 🧠 Learnings and Impact

This project wasn’t about tuning hyperparameters — it was about **thinking like a fraudster** and **designing like a defender**.

Here’s what I walked away with:

✅In fraud detection, **behavior > metadata**

✅Class imbalance isn’t a nuisance — it’s the *reality*

✅Explainable models are vital for compliance & trust

✅ML is not just about models — it’s about systems

# Case Study Files

📂GitHub Files: [Click here](https://github.com/trideep126/Business_Case_Studies/tree/main/PayPal)

💼PDF Summary: [Click here](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1G-2MP2AO_RK6atAhNJ9CVps5ZNfYrjBC/view?usp=sharing)

# ️️️✍️ ️Final Thoughts

Whether you’re working in fintech, compliance, or AI — projects like these show the real power of **data for defense**.

What started as a messy spreadsheet became a **real-time alerting system**.

If PayPal were to implement this today, they wouldn’t just be reacting to fraud — they’d be **outsmarting it**.

More coming soon !

FULL CONTENT:
# Fortify Your Business Using PayPal’s Risk and Fraud Management Solutions

![authorImage](https://www.paypalobjects.com/devdoc/profile_Dibyani.jpg)

Dibyani Das

Jun 05, 2025

6 min read

![featuredImage](https://www.paypalobjects.com/ppdevdocs/Fraud%20PS.png)

Fraud is constantly evolving, becoming not only more sophisticated but also more accessible. For every dollar lost to fraud, businesses can incur up to four dollars in total costs, factoring in chargebacks and the erosion of  
customer trust.

At PayPal, security in the way customers manage their payments is foundational. Our payment systems use AI machine learning, monitoring thousands of signals in real time to help prevent fraud before it occurs. However, the effectiveness of machine  
learning hinges on the quality of its data. Success starts with integrating clean, relevant data, beginning with signals from your payment provider and enhanced by custom fields to improve detection from the outset.

We'll explore the latest PayPal advancements that help enable you to detect fraud and secure your transactions.

## The Power of PayPal: Flexible, Intelligent Payments & Risk  Solutions

In today’s digital commerce landscape, growth requires balancing seamless customer experiences with robust security. Businesses need payment solutions that not only drive conversions but also help defend against evolving  
fraud threats. PayPal’s integrated platform excels by combining smart payment optimization with advanced, data-driven risk management.

### Smart Payments and Risk Solutions

At PayPal’s core is one of the largest and most current payment datasets, spanning over 400 million consumer accounts and 20 million merchant accounts worldwide.

This vast data reservoir powers our machine learning models, enabling smart transaction decisions.  Our intelligent fraud detection system evaluates transactions using more than 500 data points, from purchase history to device and location, generating risk scores that could detect fraud and help minimize false declines, ensuring a smooth checkout for genuine customers.

### Flexible Payments and Risk Solutions

What sets PayPal’s platform apart is its flexibility. Businesses benefit from  
an open, modular infrastructure that serves as a single point of entry for both payments and risk management. PayPal offers a suite of risk  
management products tailored to your needs, whether you require basic
fraud detection, chargeback protection, or granular risk decision-making tools.

This modularity allows you to scale efficiently, integrating the right 
level of protection as your business grows or as the threat landscape shifts.

With PayPal, businesses never have to choose between security and simplicity. Our flexible, intelligent solutions empower you to optimize  
payments and manage risk, all from a single, trusted platform.

### Fraud Protection Advanced

Fraud Protection Advanced (FPA) is PayPal’s AI-powered solution that gives risk teams flexible, real-time fraud control. It generates dynamic risk scores for each transaction and provides intelligent recommendations to approve, block, or review payments. Users can create custom filters, add relevant  
fields, and manage detailed block, review, and allow lists to align with their policies. An intuitive dashboard offers clear insights into transaction trends and  
fraud patterns, enabling teams to respond swiftly and effectively.

FPA blends automation with customization, keeping businesses ahead of fraud  
while ensuring smooth customer experiences.

### Chargeback Protection

Chargeback Protection (CBP) is PayPal’s advanced risk management  
solution designed to help businesses reduce fraud exposure and minimize the financial impact of chargebacks on eligible credit and debit card  
transactions.

Leveraging real-time fraud analysis powered by PayPal’s  
industry-leading machine learning , CBP automatically approves or declines transactions, helping prevent fraudulent activity and false declines without manual review. 
When eligible chargebacks for fraud/unauthorized or product not received  
are received, PayPal waives chargeback fees and does not debit the
disputed amount, up to your monthly loss cap, as long as required evidence 
is provided.

This predictable, scalable protection allows merchants to sell  
confidently, reduce operational overhead, and avoid costly chargeback fees. CBP is easy to enable within PayPal and Braintree, with no setup costs,  
integration fees, or risk expertise required.

The Merchant Dashboard provides real-time transparency and analytics, including dispute-level reporting and visibility into your loss cap. With CBP, you can expand to new markets, process orders faster, and keep fraud in check, helping your business grow securely and efficiently.

### Dispute Automation

Dispute Automation is PayPal’s robust solution for streamlining chargeback
management, enabling businesses to contest disputed transactions and recover lost revenue with minimal manual effort.

At the core, the solution is customizable, along with comprehensive response templates that help ensure all necessary evidence is included, improving the chances of winning disputes. Intelligent decisioning  
capabilities analyze dispute patterns and recommend which cases are worth 
contesting, helping businesses avoid wasted effort and optimize operational costs.

The system is built to align with industry standards, including support for Visa’s Compelling Evidence 3.0 framework, enhancing your ability to  
respond effectively. A low-code integration approach makes it quick and  
easy to implement, while real-time access to chargeback data enables better 
visibility and control.

PayPal has documented use cases where our risk and fraud management solutions  
have improved the financial health of organizations. Let’s look at two such cases.

## Case Study 1: Tickeri

![image](https://www.paypalobjects.com/ppdevdocs/fraud-tickeri.png "undefined")

![image](https://www.paypalobjects.com/ppdevdocs/fraud-tickeri.png "undefined")

Tickeri aimed to expand into new markets and build trust with Latino eventgoers through a seamless, mobile-first payment experience. To achieve 
these goals, they integrated PayPal’s unified commerce platform, PayPal  
Open, which delivers both low friction payments and robust fraud  
protection.

By leveraging PayPal Checkout, Pay Later, and Chargeback Protection (CBP), Tickeri enhanced the customer experience while safeguarding
revenue.

The results are compelling: fradulent card transactions dropped by 53%, chargeback disputes fell by 27.5%, and issuer declines decreased by 17%.

With PayPal’s scalable solutions, Tickeri successfully improved ticket
purchasing for fans and provided event organizers with a reliable, streamlined payment system, fueling their growth in new markets.

*Note: Certain transactions and chargebacks are not eligible for Chargeback Protection. See terms. Chargebacks that are not related to fraud or item not received (INR), such as broken Item, significantly not as described (SNAD), refund not processed, and duplicate charge, are not eligible for Chargeback Protection. Chargeback Protection is available for accounts enrolled in Advanced Credit and Debit Card Payments.*

## Case Study 2: Avelo Airlines

![image](https://www.paypalobjects.com/ppdevdocs/Fraud-Airline.png "undefined")

![image](https://www.paypalobjects.com/ppdevdocs/Fraud-Airline.png "undefined")

Avelo Airlines needed a scalable and secure payments solution to deliver a smooth checkout experience for customers. By integrating PayPal Braintree 
and Fraud Protection Advanced (FPA), they achieved stronger fraud  
management and improved transaction approval rates. As a result, Avelo saw a 3% increase in approval rates and a 15.5% reduction in chargebacks, boosting both revenue and customer satisfaction.

## Built-In Fraud Protection with PayPal

Fraud is getting smarter, but so are the tools to combat it. With PayPal’s secure-by-design approach, you gain access to a flexible suite of AI/ML- powered capabilities, all fueled by PayPal’s expansive dataset, to keep your payments safe without slowing down your customers.

The goal: make fraud protection easy so you can focus on growing your business and delighting your customers. When security is built right in, everyone wins.

For more information visit [PayPal.AI](https://www.paypal.ai/).

enriched_points.expanded_content: PayPal employs advanced machine learning models to analyze transaction patterns, effectively detecting and preventing fraud before it occurs, safeguarding billions in daily transactions.
ZestFinance utilizes machine learning to evaluate creditworthiness, ensuring fair access to loans while maintaining compliance with fair lending practices and enhanced transparency in decisions.
Both PayPal and ZestFinance adhere to strict regulations, employing explainable AI to ensure compliance with financial laws, thereby building trust with customers and regulatory bodies.
ZestFinance's approach to credit assessments promotes equitable lending by using transparent AI models that minimize bias, ensuring fair treatment for all applicants.
Explainable AI frameworks enhance transparency in financial decision-making, allowing stakeholders to understand model predictions and fostering trust in automated systems.
